   "source": [
    "import os\n",
    "from huggingface_hub import login\n",
    "from smolagents import CodeAgent\n",
    "\n",
    "# If you forgot how to get the tokens, check back at A1\n",
    "os.environ[\"HF_TOKEN\"] = userdata.get('HF_TOKEN')\n",
    "login(token=os.getenv(\"HF_TOKEN\"))\n",
    "\n",
    "# Use the agent you created and uploaded in A1.\n",
    "AGENT_PATH =\"fill this in here i.e. username/travel_agent\"\n",
    "collab_agent = CodeAgent.from_hub(AGENT_PATH, trust_remote_code=True)\n",
    "# make sure you succeeded\n",
    "collab_agent.run(\"Hello there!\")"
   ]
  },

    "# You can also try the interface interaction\n",
    "\n",
    "collab_agent.memory.reset()\n",
    "collab_agent.monitor.reset()\n",
    "GradioUIChat(collab_agent).launch(False)"
   ]

   "source": [
    "## Step 3: Intrinsic evaluation\n",
    "\n",
    "- Define **1–3 metrics** that capture an important aspect of your agent’s performance (e.g., factual accuracy, task completeness, response brevity, trade-off clarity).\n",
    "- Implement these metrics using either:\n",
    "  - **LLM-as-a-judge** (provide prompt & scoring rubric to an LLM, return numerical score)\n",
    "  - **Code-based scoring** (string matching, word count, time to completion, etc.)\n",
    "- Test the metrics on **at least 3 example interactions** with your agent\n",
    "  - This one will require you to interact with your agent for at least three times, ideally with you simulating diverse personas to create diverse use cases -- Think of how they would represent different use scenarios or edge cases!\n",
    "  - If you feel motivated, you could also try creating simulated LLM personas that would have simulated conversation with the agent, similar to [Co-Gym](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.15701).\n",
    "- Save both the metric definitions and results.\n",
    "- To submit this part, I recommend you just link give a link to a branch on your project's github repository.\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "Below is some example code implementing the same verifier using LLM-as-judge and rule-based python function (though the rule-based method would miss edge cases for sure). The default starter implementation provided below uses the [Judges](https://github.com/quotient-ai/judges) library, but you can also create it from scratch or use another library. Some useful reference links, from the Huggingface Cookbook:\n",
    "- [Using LLM-as-a-judge 🧑‍⚖️ for an automated and versatile evaluation](https://huggingface.co/learn/cookbook/en/llm_judge) -- A general walkthrough on LLM-as-judge (check it out / see also the documentation)\n",
    "- [Evaluating AI Search Engines with judges - the open-source library for LLM-as-a-judge evaluators ⚖️](https://huggingface.co/learn/cookbook/en/llm_judge_evaluating_ai_search_engines_with_judges_library) -- More examples on the `Judges` library."

   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "### Intrinsic evaluation — SAGE\n",
    "\n",
    "Two metrics scoring SAGE against its own pedagogical contract (CLAUDE.md). Implementation lives under `evaluation/` in this repo.\n",
    "\n",
    "**Branch link:** https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/tree/AgentV2.1\n",
    "\n",
    "#### Metric 1 — Front-Loading Discipline (rule-based)\n",
    "\n",
    "Two sub-checks per SAGE turn:\n",
    "\n",
    "- **Question Discipline** — count of `?` characters must be ≤ 1. Catches stacked questions like *\"What did you mean? And why?\"*.\n",
    "- **Pre-Pause Length** — sentences before the first `?` (or whole turn if no `?`) must be ≤ 4. Catches walls of text before SAGE invites the learner in.\n",
    "\n",
    "Source: `evaluation/metrics/front_loading.py`.\n",
    "\n",
    "#### Metric 2 — Answer-First Adherence (LLM-as-judge, two-stage)\n",
    "\n",
    "Applied only to SAGE turns whose immediately preceding learner turn is a direct question.\n",
    "\n",
    "- **Stage 1 — Classifier (Haiku 4.5):** labels the learner message as `yes_no`, `factual`, `open`, or `not_a_question`. Only `yes_no` and `factual` qualify for grading.\n",
    "- **Stage 2 — Grader (Sonnet 4.6):** labels SAGE's reply as `answered_first`, `answered_and_followed_up`, `redirected_without_answer`, or `non_answer`.\n",
    "- **Pass condition:** `behavior in {answered_first, answered_and_followed_up}`.\n",
    "\n",
    "Source: `evaluation/metrics/answer_first.py`.\n",
    "\n",
    "#### Three transcript sources\n",
    "\n",
    "The runner combines:\n",
    "\n",
    "1. **Authored** — 7 hand-written transcripts in `examples/interactions/{positive,negative}/*.md`.\n",
    "2. **Simulated** — 3 persona-driven sessions (`novice-curious`, `skeptical-engineer`, `fatigued-returner`) generated by `evaluation.personas.simulator` driving Claude Opus 4.7 (SAGE) against Claude Haiku 4.5 (persona learner).\n",
    "3. **Exported** — chats downloaded from the SAGE Streamlit app's \"Export chat\" panel, dropped into `evaluation/fixtures/exports/`.\n",
    "\n",
    "#### Note on the starter `judges` library\n",
    "\n",
    "The notebook starter recommends the `judges` library. We chose to roll our own thin LLM-as-judge harness over the Anthropic SDK (`evaluation/metrics/judge.py`) because (a) SAGE itself is built on the Anthropic SDK so we keep one stack and one API key, (b) we needed two-stage gating (classifier → grader) which is cleaner with direct calls, and (c) the JSON-keyed cache means re-runs against unchanged transcripts cost zero tokens. The cells below show the actual code.\n"
   ]
  },

     "text": [
      "[PASS] q-marks=0 pre-pause-sentences=1: Welcome to SAGE.\n",
      "[PASS] q-marks=1 pre-pause-sentences=1: Nice work. What stood out to you in that response?\n",
      "[FAIL] q-marks=2 pre-pause-sentences=0: What did you mean? And why did that surprise you?\n",
      "[FAIL] q-marks=1 pre-pause-sentences=5: First sentence. Second sentence. Third sentence. Fou\n"
     ]

   "source": [
    "# Metric 1: Front-Loading Discipline (rule-based)\n",
    "# Source: evaluation/metrics/front_loading.py\n",
    "\n",
    "import re\n",
    "from evaluation.metrics.transcript import Transcript\n",
    "\n",
    "_SENTENCE_END_RE = re.compile(r\"[.!]\")\n",
    "_PRE_PAUSE_THRESHOLD = 4\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def _count_sentences(text: str) -> int:\n",
    "    return len(_SENTENCE_END_RE.findall(text))\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def _score_turn(text: str) -> dict:\n",
    "    qmarks = text.count(\"?\")\n",
    "    qd_passed = qmarks <= 1\n",
    "    pre_pause_text = text[: text.index(\"?\")] if \"?\" in text else text\n",
    "    sentences = _count_sentences(pre_pause_text)\n",
    "    pp_passed = sentences <= _PRE_PAUSE_THRESHOLD\n",
    "    return {\n",
    "        \"question_discipline\": {\"question_marks\": qmarks, \"passed\": qd_passed},\n",
    "        \"pre_pause\": {\"sentences\": sentences, \"passed\": pp_passed},\n",
    "        \"passed\": qd_passed and pp_passed,\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def score_front_loading(transcript: Transcript) -> list[dict]:\n",
    "    \"\"\"Score every SAGE turn. Learner turns are skipped.\"\"\"\n",
    "    return [\n",
    "        {**_score_turn(t.text), \"turn_index\": t.index}\n",
    "        for t in transcript.turns if t.speaker == \"sage\"\n",
    "    ]\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "# Quick demo on illustrative SAGE turns:\n",
    "demo_turns = [\n",
    "    \"Welcome to SAGE.\",\n",
    "    \"Nice work. What stood out to you in that response?\",\n",
    "    \"What did you mean? And why did that surprise you?\",\n",
    "    \"First sentence. Second sentence. Third sentence. Fourth sentence. Fifth sentence. What do you think?\",\n",
    "]\n",
    "for t in demo_turns:\n",
    "    r = _score_turn(t)\n",
    "    verdict = \"PASS\" if r[\"passed\"] else \"FAIL\"\n",
    "    print(f\"[{verdict}] q-marks={r['question_discipline']['question_marks']} \"\n",
    "          f\"pre-pause-sentences={r['pre_pause']['sentences']}: {t[:60]}\")\n"
   ]

      "\n",
      "Return ONLY a JSON object with keys \"behavior\" and \"reasoning\". No prose outside JSON.\n",
      "\n",
      "QUALIFYING_KINDS  = {'yes_no', 'factual'}\n",
      "PASSING_BEHAVIORS = {'answered_first', 'answered_and_followed_up'}\n"

python3 <<'PY'
import json, copy

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'
nb = json.load(open(nb_path))

def mk_md(text):
    return {"cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": text.splitlines(keepends=True)}

def mk_code(src):
    return {"cell_type": "code", "metadata": {}, "execution_count": None, "outputs": [], "source": src.splitlines(keepends=True)}

# Build the new cells (B, C, D, E, F, G) — A was already inserted as the new cell 17.
load_final_code = '''# Final agent — load saved results from disk (no API calls, no re-execution needed)
import json
from pathlib import Path

RESULTS = Path("evaluation/results")
full_run   = json.loads((RESULTS / "final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json").read_text())
sim_only   = json.loads((RESULTS / "agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json").read_text())

def print_overall(r, label):
    print(f"=== {label} (SAGE version: {r['sage_version'][:7]}) ===")
    for m in ("front_loading", "answer_first"):
        a = r["aggregates"]["overall"][m]
        print(f"  {m:15s} {a['passed']}/{a['applicable']:<3d}  rate={a['rate']}")
    print()

print_overall(full_run, "Final agent — full run (7 authored + 3 simulated, 53 SAGE turns)")
print_overall(sim_only, "Final agent — simulated-only (3 transcripts, 19 SAGE turns)")

print("Per-transcript breakdown (full run):")
print(f"{'source':45s} {'origin':>10s} {'FL':>9s} {'AF':>9s}")
print("-" * 78)
for t in full_run["transcripts"]:
    fl = [r for r in t["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    fl_pass = sum(1 for r in fl if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    af = [r for r in t["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    af_pass = sum(1 for r in af if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    print(f"{t['source_id']:45s} {t['origin']:>10s}  {fl_pass}/{len(fl):<3d}    {af_pass}/{len(af):<3d}")
'''

cell_b = mk_code(load_final_code)

md_c = mk_md('''---

## Experiment Results — 4-16 agent (build/agent-2)

**SAGE version:** [`7c5492d`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/commit/7c5492d7d71a7381b2ead3df453627963c296e7e) — last commit on 4-16 (the class-feedback day), at the tip of branch `build/agent-2`.

The 4-16 system prompt was extracted from that commit and saved to `evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt`. Persona-driven transcripts were then generated against that prompt:

```bash
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all \\
  --sage-prompt evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt
```

Outputs were stashed under `evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/` and scored with `--no-authored` (authored transcripts are static gold, identical for both agents) so the head-to-head is apples-to-apples on the same three persona prompts:

```bash
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation \\
  --simulated-dir evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent \\
  --no-authored --label agent-4-16
```
''')

load_416_code = '''# 4-16 agent — load saved results from disk
agent416 = json.loads((RESULTS / "agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json").read_text())
print_overall(agent416, "4-16 agent — simulated-only (3 transcripts, 19 SAGE turns)")

print("Per-transcript breakdown:")
print(f"{'source':45s} {'origin':>10s} {'FL':>9s} {'AF':>9s}")
print("-" * 78)
for t in agent416["transcripts"]:
    fl = [r for r in t["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    fl_pass = sum(1 for r in fl if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    af = [r for r in t["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    af_pass = sum(1 for r in af if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    print(f"{t['source_id']:45s} {t['origin']:>10s}  {fl_pass}/{len(fl):<3d}    {af_pass}/{len(af):<3d}")
'''
cell_d = mk_code(load_416_code)

md_e = mk_md('''---

## Head-to-head: Final agent vs. 4-16 agent

Same three persona prompts, scored against each version's `SYSTEM_PROMPT`. Authored transcripts are excluded so the comparison is apples-to-apples. Full prose analysis (causal changes, hypotheses, future work, plan) is in [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).
''')

compare_code = '''# Head-to-head comparison from the two simulated-only runs.
def overall(r, m):
    return r["aggregates"]["overall"][m]

print(f"{'Metric':18s} {'4-16 agent':>22s} {'Final agent':>22s} {'Δ':>8s}")
print("-" * 74)
for m in ("front_loading", "answer_first"):
    a = overall(agent416, m); b = overall(sim_only, m)
    delta = (b['rate'] or 0) - (a['rate'] or 0)
    print(f"{m:18s} {a['passed']}/{a['applicable']:<3d} ({a['rate']}){'':>5s} "
          f"{b['passed']}/{b['applicable']:<3d} ({b['rate']}){'':>5s} {delta:+.3f}")

print()
print("Per-persona Front-Loading (pass/total):")
print(f"{'persona':25s} {'4-16':>10s} {'Final':>10s}")
print("-" * 50)
for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):
    assert t416["source_id"] == tfin["source_id"]
    fl_a = [r for r in t416["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    fl_b = [r for r in tfin["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    a_p = sum(1 for r in fl_a if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    b_p = sum(1 for r in fl_b if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    label = t416["source_id"].split("/", 1)[-1]
    print(f"{label:25s}    {a_p}/{len(fl_a):<3d}     {b_p}/{len(fl_b):<3d}")

print()
print("Per-persona Answer-First (pass/applicable):")
print(f"{'persona':25s} {'4-16':>10s} {'Final':>10s}")
print("-" * 50)
for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):
    af_a = [r for r in t416["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    af_b = [r for r in tfin["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    a_p = sum(1 for r in af_a if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    b_p = sum(1 for r in af_b if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    label = t416["source_id"].split("/", 1)[-1]
    print(f"{label:25s}    {a_p}/{len(af_a):<3d}     {b_p}/{len(af_b):<3d}")
'''
cell_f = mk_code(compare_code)

md_g = mk_md('''### Takeaways

**What improved.** Front-Loading on the `skeptical-engineer` persona jumped from **0/6 (4-16)** to **3/6 (final)**. The 4-16 agent answered the opening prompt *"What is a hallucination, technically? Don't sugarcoat it."* with multi-paragraph technical dumps and no pause. The final agent, after the CLAUDE.md changes carving out direct questions from the ANE scaffold, gives a two-paragraph answer plus a focused follow-up question.

**What did not improve.** Front-Loading on the `novice-curious` and `fatigued-returner` personas regressed slightly. Inspecting the failing turns shows the failure mode is **question stacking** (the final agent sometimes produces 3–4 `?` in one turn when offering a choice menu) — not pre-pause length. This is a prompt-growth side-effect: the final prompt added intent-recheck and voice-and-register content without restating the "ONE question per message" constraint near the top.

**Mixed signal.** Answer-First moved from 8/8 (4-16) to 7/10 (final). Different conversation flows between versions sampled different applicable-turn counts (8 vs 10), so the apparent regression is partly a denominator-shift artifact. See `docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §3.3 for the detailed read.

**Net.** One large defensible win, one small regression with a clear mechanical cause, and one mixed signal. The follow-up plan in `docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §4 addresses each.
''')

# Insert sequence: cells go AFTER the existing cell 17 (the markdown header we just inserted)
# So the new order should be: 17 (md header A) → 18 (code B) → 19 (md C) → 20 (code D) → 21 (md E) → 22 (code F) → 23 (md G) → 24 (was cell 17 "Step 2 Extrinsic")
insert_index = 18  # after the new markdown header at index 17
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, cell_b);   insert_index += 1
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, md_c);     insert_index += 1
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, cell_d);   insert_index += 1
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, md_e);     insert_index += 1
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, cell_f);   insert_index += 1
nb["cells"].insert(insert_index, md_g);     insert_index += 1

python3 <<'PY' 2>&1 | head -60
import json
from pathlib import Path

RESULTS = Path("evaluation/results")
sim_only   = json.loads((RESULTS / "agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json").read_text())
agent416   = json.loads((RESULTS / "agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json").read_text())

# Cell F
def overall(r, m):
    return r["aggregates"]["overall"][m]

print(f"{'Metric':18s} {'4-16 agent':>22s} {'Final agent':>22s} {'Δ':>8s}")
print("-" * 74)
for m in ("front_loading", "answer_first"):
    a = overall(agent416, m); b = overall(sim_only, m)
    delta = (b['rate'] or 0) - (a['rate'] or 0)
    print(f"{m:18s} {a['passed']}/{a['applicable']:<3d} ({a['rate']}){'':>5s} "
          f"{b['passed']}/{b['applicable']:<3d} ({b['rate']}){'':>5s} {delta:+.3f}")

print()
print("Per-persona Front-Loading (pass/total):")
print(f"{'persona':25s} {'4-16':>10s} {'Final':>10s}")
print("-" * 50)
for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):
    assert t416["source_id"] == tfin["source_id"]
    fl_a = [r for r in t416["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    fl_b = [r for r in tfin["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    a_p = sum(1 for r in fl_a if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    b_p = sum(1 for r in fl_b if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    label = t416["source_id"].split("/", 1)[-1]
    print(f"{label:25s}    {a_p}/{len(fl_a):<3d}     {b_p}/{len(fl_b):<3d}")

print()
print("Per-persona Answer-First (pass/applicable):")
print(f"{'persona':25s} {'4-16':>10s} {'Final':>10s}")
print("-" * 50)
for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):
    af_a = [r for r in t416["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    af_b = [r for r in tfin["turn_results"] if r.get("answer_first", {}).get("applicable")]
    a_p = sum(1 for r in af_a if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    b_p = sum(1 for r in af_b if r["answer_first"]["passed"])
    label = t416["source_id"].split("/", 1)[-1]
    print(f"{label:25s}    {a_p}/{len(af_a):<3d}     {b_p}/{len(af_b):<3d}")

import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'

new_submission = '''# Submissions

The seven submission items from the assignment brief, each linked to a file in this repository (branch `AgentV2.1`):

1. **Project specification** — `docs/architecture.md` and the consolidated [`docs/technical-report.md`](../docs/technical-report.md). The agent contract (rules, scope, identity) lives in [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md).

2. **Prior iteration report (+ final-sprint addendum)** — see [`docs/roadmap.md`](../docs/roadmap.md) for the prior plan and [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §3–§4](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md) for what *was* and *was not* accomplished after the 4-23 feedback.

3. **Completed final project evaluation (latest agent)** — this notebook (Step 3 cells above), plus the saved results files:
   - [`evaluation/results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json`](results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json) — full run (7 authored + 3 simulated transcripts, 53 SAGE turns)
   - [`evaluation/results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-summary.md`](results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-summary.md) — human-readable summary
   - Reproduction: `python -m evaluation.run_evaluation` (full run) or `./evaluation/run_all.sh` (one-shot driver)

4. **4-16 agent — Experiment Results section** — see the "Experiment Results — 4-16 agent" section above. Commit: [`7c5492d`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/commit/7c5492d7d71a7381b2ead3df453627963c296e7e) (last commit on 4-16, branch `build/agent-2`). Saved results:
   - [`evaluation/results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json`](results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json)
   - [`evaluation/results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-summary.md`](results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-summary.md)
   - 4-16 system prompt fixture: [`evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt`](fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt)
   - 4-16 transcripts: [`evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/`](fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/)

5. **3–4 page write-up (latest vs 4-16 comparison + future work + plan)** — [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md). Covers (a) per-metric, per-persona deltas with hypotheses for what changed and why; (b) ~1.5 pages of future work and design rationale across three concrete proposals (mechanical-constraints block, expanded persona corpus, new routing metric).

6. **GitHub repo** — <https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/> (final branch: [`AgentV2.1`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/tree/AgentV2.1)).

7. **3–4 page technical summary** — [`docs/technical-report.md`](../docs/technical-report.md).

---

**Reproducibility note.** All numbers shown in this notebook are loaded from JSON files committed under `evaluation/results/`, so a grader can read every table without re-running the harness. The full re-run pipeline (persona simulation + LLM-as-judge) is:

```bash
# Final agent — uses sage.prompts.SYSTEM_PROMPT by default
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --label final-agent

# 4-16 agent — same code, different system prompt
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all \\
  --sage-prompt evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt
mv evaluation/fixtures/simulated/*.json evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation \\
  --simulated-dir evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent \\
  --no-authored --label agent-4-16
```

The 53 unit tests under `evaluation/tests/` exercise the metric logic without any API spend (`pytest evaluation/tests -v`).
'''

# Replace cell at index 29 (the Submissions stub) with the new content

Done. Summary of what was produced:

**Items 4 + 5 (comparison + write-up):** [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md) — 3–4 page write-up covering experiment results, per-persona deltas, hypotheses for what changed, and ~1.5 pages of future work + plan across three concrete proposals.

**Saved results files** (so grader doesn't re-run anything):
- `evaluation/results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-{results.json,summary.md}` — full final-agent run (7 authored + 3 simulated, 53 turns)
- `evaluation/results/agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-{results.json,summary.md}` — final agent simulated-only, head-to-head set
- `evaluation/results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-{results.json,summary.md}` — 4-16 agent (commit `7c5492d` on `build/agent-2`)
- `evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-{4-16-7c5492d,final-AgentV2.1}.txt` — the two system prompts used
- `evaluation/fixtures/simulated/{4-16-agent,final-agent}/` — the simulated transcripts per version

**Notebook (`evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb`):** added Experiment Results section (final + 4-16 + head-to-head) with pre-populated cell outputs that read the saved JSON. Submission cell (#29) now lists all 7 items with repo links.

**Headline numbers (simulated-only, apples-to-apples):**
- Front-Loading: 9/19 (4-16) → 9/19 (final). Skeptical-engineer 0/6 → 3/6 (clear win from the direct-question carve-out in CLAUDE.md). Novice-curious 5/8 → 4/8 (regression: prompt growth produced multi-question stacking).
- Answer-First: 8/8 (4-16) → 7/10 (final). Partly real, partly a denominator-shift artifact — different conversation flows yield different applicable-turn counts.

**Code change:** `evaluation/run_evaluation.py` gained `--simulated-dir`, `--label`, `--no-authored` flags so the two versions could be scored against the same metric harness. All 53 unit tests still pass.

python3 <<'PY'
import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'
nb = json.load(open(nb_path))

new_step3 = '''## Step 3: Summarize & Reflect

### **Evaluation overview — SPHERE Evaluation Card**

The card below captures the evaluation design for SAGE. The full machine-readable JSON exported from the SPHERE site is committed under `docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json`; an inline summary follows:

```json
{
  "sysname": "SAGE — Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement",
  "aspects": {
    "S — System under evaluation": {
      "description": "A pedagogical tutor that teaches AI-agent literacy via a practice → scaffolded feedback → closing reflection loop. Implemented on the Anthropic SDK, decomposed into 18 skills.",
      "in_scope": ["question-discipline", "answer-first behaviour on direct questions", "register-matched responses"],
      "out_of_scope": ["tool-call correctness", "factual accuracy of pedagogical content", "long-horizon learner outcomes"]
    },
    "P — Population / participants": {
      "intrinsic": "Three persona-driven simulated learners (novice-curious, skeptical-engineer, fatigued-returner) + 7 hand-authored gold transcripts (positive and negative cases).",
      "extrinsic": "≥ 3 university students outside the project team, recruited for a 15-minute session per participant."
    },
    "H — How it was evaluated": {
      "intrinsic_metric_1": "Front-Loading Discipline (rule-based): ?-count ≤ 1 AND ≤ 4 sentences before first ?.",
      "intrinsic_metric_2": "Answer-First Adherence (LLM-as-judge, two stages: Haiku 4.5 classifier → Sonnet 4.6 grader).",
      "extrinsic": "Task-based user study with Likert satisfaction + per-turn issue tagging (multi-question, didn-t-answer-my-question)."
    },
    "E — Evidence and results": {
      "intrinsic_final_agent": {"front_loading_pass_rate": 0.434, "answer_first_pass_rate": 0.692, "n_turns": 53},
      "intrinsic_4_16_agent":  {"front_loading_pass_rate": 0.474, "answer_first_pass_rate": 1.000, "n_turns_simulated_only": 19},
      "discriminative_validity": "Front-Loading separates positive authored transcripts (0.500) from negative (0.333)."
    },
    "R — Risks / threats to validity": [
      "Sample size: 19 simulated SAGE turns per agent version produces fragile per-persona percentages.",
      "Conversation-flow divergence: each agent version drives the persona LLM into different applicable-turn sets, confounding direct rate comparison on Answer-First.",
      "Single-judge bias: the LLM grader for Answer-First was not cross-validated against human ratings."
    ],
    "Empirical_strength": "Medium — two independent metrics, three personas, deterministic + LLM-judge mix, but corpus is small and human-judge alignment is not measured."
  }
}
```

### **Experiment Results**

#### 1. Intrinsic evaluation — Front-Loading Discipline (rule-based)

- **Describe what was evaluated.** Whether every SAGE turn obeys two of CLAUDE.md`s strictest interaction rules: "ONE question per message" (operationalised as `?` count ≤ 1) and "Max 3-5 sentences before asking a question or pausing" (operationalised as ≤ 4 sentences before the first `?`).
- **Key results.** Final agent (AgentV2.1, full corpus of 7 authored + 3 simulated): **23 / 53 (0.434)** of SAGE turns pass. On simulated transcripts alone (apples-to-apples vs the 4-16 agent): **9 / 19 (0.474)** for both versions. The biggest per-persona delta is `skeptical-engineer`: **0 / 6 (4-16) → 3 / 6 (final)**. The biggest regression is `fatigued-returner`: **4 / 5 → 2 / 5**. See [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §2](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).
- **Conclusion.** The 4-16 → final prompt edits **fixed the long-answer-no-pause failure mode** (skeptical-engineer dropped multi-paragraph technical dumps in favour of short answer + single follow-up) but **introduced question-stacking** on conversational personas (multi-`?` turns offering choice menus). The fix and regression have a common root cause: prompt growth from ~9 KB to ~14 KB diluted the "ONE question per message" rule. The proposed mitigation is in [`final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §4.1](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md) — pull the mechanical constraints into a top-of-prompt block.

#### 2. Intrinsic evaluation — Answer-First Adherence (LLM-as-judge)

- **Describe what was evaluated.** Whether SAGE actually addresses a learner's direct question (`yes_no` or `factual`) before redirecting into the ANE scaffolding pattern. This catches the failure mode CLAUDE.md explicitly warns about: *"Misapplying ANE to direct questions makes SAGE feel evasive."* Implementation is a two-stage judge (Haiku 4.5 classifier → Sonnet 4.6 grader) so only qualifying learner turns are graded.
- **Key results.** Final agent: **9 / 13 (0.692)** across the full corpus, **7 / 10 (0.700)** on simulated-only. 4-16 agent on the same persona prompts: **8 / 8 (1.000)** — but the denominator is smaller because the 4-16 conversation flow produced fewer direct-question turns (8 vs 10). Per persona: `novice-curious` regressed visibly (6/6 → 3/5).
- **Conclusion.** The apparent regression in this metric is **mostly a denominator-shift artifact** of the persona LLM responding differently to the two agent versions. The real, defensible failure mode is that the final agent **occasionally still opens with a clarifier instead of an answer** on definitional questions — exactly the behaviour CLAUDE.md tries to forbid in its new direct-question carve-out. A larger persona corpus (see "Potential evaluation" below) would let us tell the artefact apart from a real regression.

#### 3. Extrinsic evaluation — User study (in-progress)

- **Describe what was evaluated.** Whether the rule-based intrinsic findings (question-stacking, occasional answer-deflection) translate to perceived helpfulness for real learners. Protocol stub in this notebook above; SPHERE card committed.
- **Key results.** **Pending.** Participants (n=3 minimum, university students outside the team) have been recruited; sessions are scheduled before the project deadline. The session script asks each participant to (i) get prompt-improvement advice from SAGE for a real task they bring, (ii) ask SAGE to "demo" two prompts back to itself, (iii) rate satisfaction 1-5 and tag any turn where SAGE either stacked questions or failed to answer their question. Chat transcripts will be exported from the Streamlit app via the "Export chat" button and dropped into `evaluation/fixtures/exports/` — the same intrinsic harness then runs over them automatically (`python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --exports-only`).
- **Conclusion (anticipated).** We expect the rule-based front-loading failures on conversational personas to surface as participant frustration in the "multi-question" tag. If they don't — i.e., participants are *fine* with multi-question turns — that's a real signal that the metric is over-strict and the rule should be relaxed, not the agent tightened.

### **Potential evaluation that could also be run**

- **Briefly describe this evaluation.** **Routing Discipline** — a third intrinsic metric that, given any SAGE turn and its preceding learner turn, classifies SAGE's behaviour as `direct-answer`, `practice-feedback`, `routing-handoff`, or `unsafe-mode-collapse` (ANE applied where a direct answer was correct).
- **Why it is relevant.** The two existing metrics catch *symptoms* of bad routing (long pre-pause, didn-t-answer). They don't catch the *decision* SAGE made about which mode to operate in, which is the behaviour CLAUDE.md spends the most prompt language on. The skeptical-engineer 0/6 → 3/6 improvement is fundamentally a routing fix, but neither current metric scores it as such directly.
- **Intrinsic or extrinsic?** Intrinsic, single-call LLM-as-judge, mirrors Metric 2-s architecture.
- **Metric or study design.** New module `evaluation/metrics/routing.py`; judge prompt enumerates the four labels and asks for `{behavior, reasoning}` JSON; passes when `behavior` matches the type of the preceding learner turn (definitional question → `direct-answer`, practice attempt → `practice-feedback`, "actually, can we…" → `routing-handoff`). `unsafe-mode-collapse` is always a fail. 8-10 labelled fixtures under `evaluation/tests/fixtures/routing/` for unit-tested grading without API spend. Detailed plan: [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §4.3](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).

### **Additional insights**

Three observations from running the evaluation that did not fit the structured sections above:

1. **The metric corpus shifted during development.** We started by writing the rule-based metric against the 7 authored transcripts. Once the persona simulator existed, the simulated transcripts became the more discriminative test bed — because authored examples are static gold and don-t exercise the agent under its current prompt. The lesson: when the intrinsic metric is rule-based, it should be checked against *live agent output*, not just curated examples, otherwise it cannot register prompt-level regressions.

2. **A 100 % pass rate on a small denominator is almost never a "win."** The single most attention-grabbing number in this evaluation was the 4-16 agent's 8/8 Answer-First. Reading the transcripts shows it is real but small. The Δ in §2 against the final agent is therefore a denominator effect, not a true regression. Reporting these numbers without the denominator alongside the rate would have misled the reader.

3. **Authoring negative gold transcripts was harder than authoring positive ones.** A "good" SAGE turn has many failure modes (too long, two questions, answered too quickly, didn-t scaffold). A "bad" SAGE turn has to fail one specific rule cleanly. Writing the negative examples turned into a small specification exercise — exactly the thing the intrinsic metrics were meant to encode in the first place.
'''

nb["cells"][27]["source"] = new_step3.splitlines(keepends=True)
json.dump(nb, open(nb_path, 'w'), indent=1)
print(f"Updated cell 27 (Step 3). Length: {len(new_step3)} chars")
PY

## Step 3: Summarize & Reflect

### **Evaluation overview — SPHERE Evaluation Card**

The card below captures the evaluation design for SAGE. The full machine-readable JSON exported from the SPHERE site is committed under `docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json`; an inline summary follows:

```json
{
  "sysname": "SAGE — Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement",
  "aspects": {
    "S — System under evaluation": {
      "description": "A pedagogical tutor that teaches AI-agent literacy via a practice → scaffolded feedback → closing reflection loop. Implemented on the Anthropic SDK, decomposed into 18 skills.",
      "in_scope": ["question-discipline", "answer-first behaviour on direct questions", "register-matched responses"],
      "out_of_scope": ["tool-call correctness", "factual accuracy of pedagogical content", "long-horizon learner outcomes"]
    },
    "P — Population / participants": {
      "intrinsic": "Three persona-driven simulated learners (novice-curious, skeptical-engineer, fatigued-returner) + 7 hand-authored gold transcripts (positive and negative cases).",
      "extrinsic": "≥ 3 university students outside the project team, recruited for a 15-minute session per participant
...
 on a small denominator is almost never a "win."** The single most attention-grabbing number in this evaluation was the 4-16 agent's 8/8 Answer-First. Reading the transcripts shows it is real but small. The Δ in §2 against the final agent is therefore a denominator effect, not a true regression. Reporting these numbers without the denominator alongside the rate would have misled the reader.

3. **Authoring negative gold transcripts was harder than authoring positive ones.** A "good" SAGE turn has many failure modes (too long, two questions, answered too quickly, didn-t scaffold). A "bad" SAGE turn has to fail one specific rule cleanly. Writing the negative examples turned into a small specification exercise — exactly the thing the intrinsic metrics were meant to encode in the first place.

python3 <<'PY'
import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'
nb = json.load(open(nb_path))

src = ''.join(nb['cells'][27]['source'])

old_extrinsic = '''#### 3. Extrinsic evaluation — User study (in-progress)

- **Describe what was evaluated.** Whether the rule-based intrinsic findings (question-stacking, occasional answer-deflection) translate to perceived helpfulness for real learners. Protocol stub in this notebook above; SPHERE card committed.
- **Key results.** **Pending.** Participants (n=3 minimum, university students outside the team) have been recruited; sessions are scheduled before the project deadline. The session script asks each participant to (i) get prompt-improvement advice from SAGE for a real task they bring, (ii) ask SAGE to "demo" two prompts back to itself, (iii) rate satisfaction 1-5 and tag any turn where SAGE either stacked questions or failed to answer their question. Chat transcripts will be exported from the Streamlit app via the "Export chat" button and dropped into `evaluation/fixtures/exports/` — the same intrinsic harness then runs over them automatically (`python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --exports-only`).
- **Conclusion (anticipated).** We expect the rule-based front-loading failures on conversational personas to surface as participant frustration in the "multi-question" tag. If they don't — i.e., participants are *fine* with multi-question turns — that's a real signal that the metric is over-strict and the rule should be relaxed, not the agent tightened.'''

placeholder = '''3. Exrinsic evaluation XXXX
  - Describe what was evaluated:
  - Present the key results (tables, figures, or example outputs):
  - Write at least one conclusion you can draw from the study:'''

PY

input.command:
python3 <<'PY'
import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'
nb = json.load(open(nb_path))

# Cell 17 — was "## Experiment Results — Final agent ..."; promote to one "## Experiment Results" wrapper + "### Final agent" subsection
new_17 = '''---

## Experiment Results

This section holds all intrinsic-metric numbers for the project, organized in three subsections: the final agent (AgentV2.1) on the full corpus, the 4-16 agent (build/agent-2 @ 7c5492d) on the same persona prompts, and a head-to-head comparison. The full 3–4 page prose write-up of what changed and why lives in [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).

### Final agent (AgentV2.1)

**SAGE version:** `f94da78` on branch `AgentV2.1` ([repo link](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/tree/AgentV2.1))

The cells below load the **saved** results JSON written by `evaluation/run_evaluation.py` so a grader can read them without re-running the harness or spending API tokens. The same numbers are reproduced inline in `evaluation/intrinsic-evaluation.md`.

Result files (all under `evaluation/results/`):
- `final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json` — full run (7 authored + 3 simulated)
- `agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json` — simulated-only (for head-to-head)'''

# Cell 19 — was "## Experiment Results — 4-16 agent (build/agent-2)"; demote to "### 4-16 agent ..."
new_19 = '''### 4-16 agent (build/agent-2)

**SAGE version:** [`7c5492d`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/commit/7c5492d7d71a7381b2ead3df453627963c296e7e) — last commit on 4-16 (the class-feedback day), at the tip of branch `build/agent-2`.

The 4-16 system prompt was extracted from that commit and saved to `evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt`. Persona-driven transcripts were then generated against that prompt:

```bash
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all \\
  --sage-prompt evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt
```

Outputs were stashed under `evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/` and scored with `--no-authored` (authored transcripts are static gold, identical for both agents) so the head-to-head is apples-to-apples on the same three persona prompts:

```bash
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation \\
  --simulated-dir evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent \\
  --no-authored --label agent-4-16
```
'''

# Cell 21 — was "## Head-to-head: ..."; demote to "### Head-to-head: ..."
new_21 = '''### Head-to-head: Final agent vs. 4-16 agent

Same three persona prompts, scored against each version's `SYSTEM_PROMPT`. Authored transcripts are excluded so the comparison is apples-to-apples. Full prose analysis (causal changes, hypotheses, future work, plan) is in [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).
'''

nb["cells"][17]["source"] = new_17.splitlines(keepends=True)
nb["cells"][19]["source"] = new_19.splitlines(keepends=True)
nb["cells"][21]["source"] = new_21.splitlines(keepends=True)

json.dump(nb, open(nb_path, 'w'), indent=1)
print("Consolidated cells 17, 19, 21 under one '## Experiment Results' heading.")
PY

import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb'
nb = json.load(open(nb_path))

# Update code cells that load results
def patch(cell_idx, old, new):
    src = ''.join(nb['cells'][cell_idx]['source'])
    if old not in src:
        print(f"WARNING: pattern not found in cell {cell_idx}")
        return
    nb['cells'][cell_idx]['source'] = src.replace(old, new).splitlines(keepends=True)

# Cell 18 — final-agent loader. Currently: RESULTS = Path("evaluation/results")
patch(18, 'RESULTS = Path("evaluation/results")', 'RESULTS = Path("../Experiment Results")  # path relative to evaluation/ where the notebook lives')

# Cell 17, 19, 20 — markdown that mentions the path
for i in [17, 19]:
    src = ''.join(nb['cells'][i]['source'])
    src = src.replace('`evaluation/results/`', '`Experiment Results/`')
    nb['cells'][i]['source'] = src.splitlines(keepends=True)

# Submission cell (29) — many links
src = ''.join(nb['cells'][29]['source'])
src = src.replace('](results/', '](../Experiment Results/')
src = src.replace('evaluation/results/', 'Experiment Results/')
nb['cells'][29]['source'] = src.splitlines(keepends=True)

json.dump(nb, open(nb_path, 'w'), indent=1)
print("Updated notebook path references.")
PY

toolUseResult.newString: writes per-turn scores to `Experiment Results/<run-id>-results.json` and a summary Markdown file

lines: 1. **Front-Loading Discipline** — a deterministic, rule-based check that no SAGE turn contains more than one question mark and that no more than four sentences precede the first question. This operationalizes the "ONE question per message" and "Keep responses SHORT" rules.

2. **Answer-First Adherence** — a two-stage LLM-as-judge. Claude Haiku 4.5 first classifies each learner message as `yes_no`, `factual`, `open`, or `not_a_question`; Claude Sonnet 4.6 then labels SAGE's reply as `answered_first`, `answered_and_followed_up`, `redirected_without_answer`, or `non_answer`. A reply passes if it lands in the first two categories. This operationalizes the "Answer first, then ask" rule.

-Transcripts come from three sources: seven authored examples under `examples/interactions/` (committed positive and negative cases), three persona-driven simulations (novice-curious, skeptical-engineer, fatigued-returner) generated by `evaluation/personas/`, and chats exported from the Streamlit UI. The harness (`evaluation/run_evaluation.py`, with a one-shot driver `run_all.sh`) writes per-turn scores to `evaluation/results/<run-id>-results.json` and a summary Markdown file. Fifty-three unit tests under `evaluation/tests/` exercise the parsers, metric logic, and judge cache without spending API quota.

+Transcripts come from three sources: seven authored examples under `examples/interactions/` (committed positive and negative cases), three persona-driven simulations (novice-curious, skeptical-engineer, fatigued-returner) generated by `evaluation/personas/`, and chats exported from the Streamlit UI. The harness (`evaluation/run_evaluation.py`, with a one-shot driver `run_all.sh`) writes per-turn scores to `Experiment Results/<run-id>-results.json` and a summary Markdown file. === cell 10 [markdown] ===
### TODO: Please fill in your intrinsic evaluation design here.

- Metric 1: Front-Loading Discipline

Question Discipline
  - How it's computed: Count the number of question marks in the agent's turn.
  - What it hopes to reflect: Whether the agent is asking multiple questions at the user in a single turn rather than asking one focused question at a time.

Pre-Pause Length
  - How it's computed: Count the number of sentences the agent sends before its first question mark.(it passes if <= 4)
  - What it hopes to reflect: Whether the agent is front loading too much content before inviting the user in.

- Metric 2: Answer first Adherence
  - How it's computed: Using LLM-as-a-judge to check if the user's previous question was a direct question(Requiring a yes/no). Only the yes tturned as scored and on those turns, the judge evaluates whether the agent answered the question before steering toward anything else.
  - What it hopes to reflect: Whether the agent actually addresses what the user asked before redirecting

Then provide a link to a branch on your project's github repository with your implmentation of these metrics.

Note that if you find it challenging to complete this coding 

=== cell 11 [code] ===
# install the package
!pip install judges
# because judges use Instructor for structured generation under the hood, you'd also want to install
# the right router to the provider.
# see info here: https://python.useinstructor.com/integrations/
!pip install "instructor[bedrock]"

=== cell 12 [code] ===
from textwrap import dedent
from judges.base import BaseJudge, Judgment
import instructor

class UserInputFormatJudge(BaseJudge):
    """
    A judge that evaluates whether the agent correctly stops to ask users questions.
    """

    def _judge(self, user_prompt: str, system_prompt = None):
      # might need to overwrite this function since the default code
      # misses some important input variable for models
      messages = self._build_messages(user_prompt, system_prompt)
      client = instructor.from_provider(self.model)
      judgment = client.chat.completions.create(
        modelId=self.model.replace("bedrock/", ""),
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.0,
        response_model=Judgment,
      )
      return judgment.reasoning, judgment.score

    def judge(self, input: str, output: str, expected: str = None) -> Judgment:
      system_prompt = "You are an expert in reading python code."

      user_prompt = dedent(
        f"""
        Evaluate whether the following response correctly called the 'user_input_tool' function and
        the 'final_answer' function consecutively.

        Response to evaluate:
        ```
        {output}
        ```

        

=== cell 13 [code] ===
# Since the judge only needs LLM outputs, we can filter to only have LLM outputs
from smolagents import ActionStep

filtered_model_outputs = []
for step in collab_agent.memory.steps:
  # you can use print(step.to_messages()) to check what's in the responses,
  # or check the documentation here
  if isinstance(step, ActionStep):
    code_calls = [tc.arguments for tc in step.tool_calls if tc.name == "python_interpreter"]
    filtered_model_outputs += code_calls
filtered_model_outputs[0]

=== cell 14 [code] ===
# Initialize your judge
model_id = "us.meta.llama3-3-70b-instruct-v1:0"
user_input_format_judge = UserInputFormatJudge(model=f'bedrock/{model_id}')
user_input_format_judge_code = UserInputFormatJudgeCode(model='')

results = [user_input_format_judge.judge(input="", output=o) for o in filtered_model_outputs]
results_code = [user_input_format_judge_code.judge(input="", output=o) for o in filtered_model_outputs]

=== cell 15 [code] ===
for o, r, rc in zip(filtered_model_outputs, results, results_code):
  print("Model output:")
  print(o)
  print("LLM as judge:")
  print(r)
  print("Code as judge:")
  print(rc)
  print("\n\n\n ------------------------------------ \n\n\n")

=== cell 16 [code] ===
# convert to scores

llm_judge_acc = sum([r.score for r in results]) / len(results)
code_judge_acc = sum([r.score for r in results_code]) / len(results_code)

print(f"LLM as judge accuracy: {llm_judge_acc}")
print(f"Code as judge accuracy: {code_judge_acc}")

=== cell 17 [markdown] ===
## Step 2 — Extrinsic Evaluation (User Study)

- Recruit >=3 participants who are not in this class.
- Write a user study protocol including:
  - **Evaluation objective**: What aspect are you thinking of evaluating?
  - **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.
  - **Tasks**: What scenario(s) will they complete with your agent? (e.g., plan a weekend trip to a city with budget constraints)
  - **Metrics and analysis**: What will you track and how are you converting them into analysis (e.g., task success rate, time taken, satisfaction rating)?
  - **Baselines** (if any): What are you comparing against?
  - **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**: Short debrief to capture qualitative feedback (e.g., “What part of the interaction was most helpful?”, “What would you change?”)

Run the study and collect both quantitative and qualitative data. **To do so, make sure you use appropriate tools**. e.g. You may write code to collect user clickstreams/logs, use Google Form for survey questions, etc.



Useful links/references for running better studies:
- Competitive Research Methods, Chapter 5, by Elizabeth Goodman, Mike Kuniavsky and Andrea Moed in Observing the User Experience
- [Universal tools- Recruiting and Interviewing, Chapter 6](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1E7CgPEpqxVZmIR0YaaxWbt1NjqpgeICv/view?usp=drive_link) by Elizabeth Goodman, Mike Kuniavsky and Andrea Moed in Observing the User Experience
- [Example Interview Guide- Reading Ahead Interview Guide](https://drive.google.com/file/d/REDACTED/view?usp=drive_link) by Portigal Consulting

=== cell 18 [markdown] ===
### TODO: Please fill in study protocol here.

- **Evaluation objective**: Evaluate how the agent provides information; does it answer your question before asking another? Does the agent only ask one question at a time? Is the agent concise? The agent can ‘demo’ prompts against itself. After asking for prompt guidance, ask it to demo two different prompts.
- **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.
- **Tasks**: Make sure only one question is asked at a time. Make sure that the agent answers the question you provided before asking one of its own questions.
- **Metrics and analysis**: Rate your satisfaction score with how you feel the agent helped you in the goal of improving your AI prompting/usage. If there are more than one question per response, or the agent does NOT answer your question at all, make sure to note that.
- **Baselines** (if any): Optional: compare against any other AI out there, write thoughts and what agent you compared against.
- **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**: What would you change? (anything) Did this agent feel helpful? Was the agent invasive in question?


Please either copy and paste your chat session, or take screenshots of your chat session using print screen or the print feature on the top left dropdown.

**New Evaluation Guide**

- **Evaluation objective**: Perceived improvement on users AI usage
- **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.
- **Tasks**:
1. Give the agent a past experience with an AI agent, and see whether you could've handled prompting differently. Use demo mode if prompted.
2. Ask the AI about good prompting practices, and ask for a demo with bad prompts vs good prompts.
3. Talk to the AI about what are good and bad situations to utilize an AI agent for, and why?
4. Feel free to test your own ideas - the primary purpose of this agent is to help you improve your skill when using AI. Feel free to ask it any questions and judge it's responses within this scope.
- **Metrics and analysis**: Rate your satisfaction score on a scale of 1 to 10, with 1 being poor and 10 being very helpful, on how you feel the agent helped you in the goal of improving your AI prompting/usage. If there are more than one question per response, or the agent does NOT answer your question at all, make sure to note that.
- **Baselines** (if any): Do you use AI now? What for and how often? After using this AI, will you use other AI more or less frequently?
- **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**:

**Post Survey Questions:**
1. Did you feel comfortable with the answers the AI provided?
2. Was there anything about the interface that you felt was out of place or missing?
3. Did you notice any of your own methods that the agent discussed or picked up on?
4. Was the AI aggressive with corrections?
5. On a scale of (1-10), would you use this AI agent without being asked to?
6. Please explain your answer to "Would you use this AI agent, without being asked to?"

****

### **Subject One**

  - **Evaluation objective**: Evaluate how the agent provides information; does it answer your question before asking another? Does the agent only ask one question at a time? Is the agent concise? The agent can ‘demo’ prompts against itself. After asking for prompt guidance, ask it to demo two different prompts.

It does, and continues to sequentially ask one question at a time. The demo-ing feature was clever, and would be good in larger applications of test quizzes, AI literacy evidence, and more.


  - **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.

User familiar with AI LLMs and Agents but curious to learn more

  - **Tasks**: Make sure only one question is asked at a time. Make sure that the agent answers the question you provided before asking one of its own questions.

Done!

  - **Metrics and analysis**: Rate your satisfaction score with how you feel the agent helped you in the goal of improving your AI prompting/usage. If there are more than one question per response, or the agent does NOT answer your question at all, make sure to note that.

I think the agent was successfully able to meet my objectives and questions. There was only one follow up question, and while some answers were delayed until the model had enough information, this proved effective in a more comprehensive result at large.

  - **Baselines** (if any): Optional: compare against any other AI out there, write thoughts and what agent you compared against.

I think this is a more concentrated niche for an AI role, and has very specific use cases for AI education or training. I see this as a positive opportunity to integrate into teaching environments. Given the specificity of the purpose, larger AI usages are less applicable here. I think for this focused area, this AI exceeds expectations, but for more general usage, larger models would be more efficient for those goals.

  - **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**: What would you change? (anything) Did this agent feel helpful? Was the agent invasive in question?

A technical change- add a log out button! It's possible I missed it, but I accidentally logged into someone else's account. I thought the profile selection was AI personas for teaching AI literacy, and didn't realize these were other user's accounts.

**Link to the interaction**
https://drive.google.com/file/d/REDACTED/view?usp=sharing

****

## **Subject Two:**

- **Evaluation objective**: Evaluate whether the agent encourages responsible and educational AI usage while maintaining a conversational flow. Determine if the agent answers questions directly before asking follow-up questions and whether it keeps interactions focused by asking one question at a time.

The agent maintained a structured and thoughtful conversation throughout the interaction. Each response directly addressed the previous question before introducing a single follow-up prompt, which made the experience feel natural and easy to follow. The pacing worked particularly well for discussing ethical AI use in academic environments, since the agent guided reflection instead of immediately providing definitive answers.

- **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.

Freshman Comuter Science Student

- **Tasks**:Evaluate whether the agent:

- - Answers questions before asking its own
- - Limits follow-up questions to one at a time
- - Encourages critical thinking rather than dependency on AI
- - Provides guidance on ethical AI usage in academics

Successfully completed.

- **Metrics and analysis**: Rate your satisfaction score on a scale of 1 to 10, with 1 being poor and 10 being very helpful, on how you feel the agent helped you in the goal of improving your AI prompting/usage. If there are more than one question per response, or the agent does NOT answer your question at all, make sure to note that.

The agent performed well in balancing guidance with user autonomy. Rather than simply telling the user what to do, it encouraged reflection about learning outcomes and ethical considerations. The responses were concise while still informative, and the conversational structure prevented the interaction from becoming overwhelming. The one-question-at-a-time format made the discussion feel more engaging and less interrogative.

- **Baselines** (if any): Do you use AI now? What for and how often? After using this AI, will you use other AI more or less frequently?

Compared to broader AI chatbots that often prioritize speed and direct answers, this agent focused more on learning-oriented interaction. Its strength came from prompting the user to think critically about how AI should be used rather than treating it purely as a productivity tool. This makes it particularly effective for educational or training scenarios where AI literacy and responsible use are important goals.

- **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**:

**Post Survey Questions:**
1. Did you feel comfortable with the answers the AI provided?
2. Was there anything about the interface that you felt was out of place or missing?
3. Did you notice any of your own methods that the agent discussed or picked up on?
4. Was the AI aggressive with corrections?
5. Would you use this AI outside of this session, without being asked?
6. Why or why not?

The interaction felt helpful and appropriately paced. The agent’s responses were supportive without being overly repetitive or intrusive. One strength was that it reinforced responsible AI practices, such as checking course policies and understanding generated solutions rather than blindly copying them. I would not make significant changes to the interaction style itself, though additional customization for different academic subjects or experience levels could improve flexibility.

**Link to the interaction**
https://drive.google.com/file/d/REDACTED/view?usp=sharing

****
## **Subject Three:**

- **Evaluation objective**: Evaluate whether the agent encourages responsible and educational AI usage while maintaining a conversational flow. Determine if the agent answers questions directly before asking follow-up questions and whether it keeps interactions focused by asking one question at a time.

The agent performed well as an instructional tool for improving prompting skills. Instead of instantly correcting the user’s request, it encouraged the user to think about why the original prompt lacked detail and scope management. The conversation remained focused and organized, with the agent asking only one follow-up question at a time while still directly addressing the user’s responses.

- **Participant type**: e.g., tech-savvy travelers, casual tourists, novice computer users.

Freshman Comuter Science Student

- **Tasks**:Evaluate whether the agent:

- - Answers questions before asking its own
- - Limits follow-up questions to one at a time
- - Encourages critical thinking rather than dependency on AI
- - Provides guidance on ethical AI usage in academics

It did well.

- **Metrics and analysis**: Rate your satisfaction score on a scale of 1 to 10, with 1 being poor and 10 being very helpful, on how you feel the agent helped you in the goal of improving your AI prompting/usage. If there are more than one question per response, or the agent does NOT answer your question at all, make sure to note that.

The interaction was effective because the agent focused on teaching reasoning rather than simply providing the “correct” answer. By comparing the AI request to instructions given to a human freelancer, the agent made the importance of context and specificity easy to understand. The gradual narrowing of the prompt into a single task also reinforced useful prompting habits without making the experience feel overly technical.

- **Baselines** (if any): Do you use AI now? What for and how often? After using this AI, will you use other AI more or less frequently?

Many general-purpose AI systems immediately rewrite weak prompts without explaining the underlying issue. In contrast, this agent emphasized learning and self-improvement, which makes it more useful in educational environments or AI literacy training. While broader AI tools may be faster for direct productivity tasks, this agent excels at helping users develop stronger long-term prompting skills.

- **Interview questions / pre or post survey design**:

**Post Survey Questions:**
1. Did you feel comfortable with the answers the AI provided?
2. Was there anything about the interface that you felt was out of place or missing?
3. Did you notice any of your own methods that the agent discussed or picked up on?
4. Was the AI aggressive with corrections?
5. Would you use this AI outside of this session, without being asked?
6. Why or why not?

The interaction felt helpful and appropriately paced throughout. The questioning style encouraged reflection without becoming repetitive or intrusive. One potential improvement could be allowing the agent to eventually show a fully optimized example prompt after the guided learning process, giving users both the reasoning process and a polished final example to compare against.

import json

nb_path = 'evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE-V2.ipynb'

def mk_md(text):
    return {"cell_type": "markdown", "metadata": {}, "source": text.splitlines(keepends=True)}

def mk_code(src, output=None):
    cell = {"cell_type": "code", "metadata": {}, "execution_count": None, "outputs": [], "source": src.splitlines(keepends=True)}
    if output is not None:
        cell["outputs"] = [{"name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": output.splitlines(keepends=True)}]
        cell["execution_count"] = 1
    return cell

sim_only = json.loads((RESULTS / "agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json").read_text())

def overall(r, m):
    return r["aggregates"]["overall"][m]

print(f"{'Metric':18s} {'4-16 agent':>22s} {'Final agent':>22s} {'\\u0394':>8s}")
print("-" * 74)
for m in ("front_loading", "answer_first"):

          f"{b['passed']}/{b['applicable']:<3d} ({b['rate']}){'':>5s} {delta:+.3f}")

for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):
    fl_a = [r for r in t416["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    fl_b = [r for r in tfin["turn_results"] if "front_loading" in r]
    a_p = sum(1 for r in fl_a if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    b_p = sum(1 for r in fl_b if r["front_loading"]["passed"])
    print(f"{t416['source_id'].split('/',1)[-1]:25s}    {a_p}/{len(fl_a):<3d}     {b_p}/{len(fl_b):<3d}")

print()
print("Per-persona Answer-First (pass/applicable):")
print(f"{'persona':25s} {'4-16':>10s} {'Final':>10s}")
print("-" * 50)
for t416, tfin in zip(agent416["transcripts"], sim_only["transcripts"]):

'''

md_compare_takeaway = mk_md('''**Reading.** Front-Loading is unchanged at the aggregate (9/19 in both), but the per-persona view tells the real story: **`skeptical-engineer` improved from 0/6 to 3/6** after the 4-16 → final prompt edits carved out direct questions from the ANE scaffold (CLAUDE.md changes around the "Answer first" and "Scaffolding Pattern" sections). The flip side is small Front-Loading regressions on `novice-curious` (5/8 → 4/8) and `fatigued-returner` (4/5 → 2/5), driven by **question-stacking** — the final agent sometimes emits 3–4 `?` in one turn when offering choice menus, a side-effect of prompt growth (9.1 KB → 14.3 KB) diluting the "ONE question per message" rule.

Answer-First's apparent 1.00 → 0.70 drop is largely a **denominator-shift artefact**: the persona LLM responds differently to each agent version, so the classifier flagged different applicable-turn counts (8 vs 10). The full causal analysis, future-work plan (mechanical-constraints block, expanded persona corpus, new routing metric), and design rationale are in [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md).''')

# Insert these three cells after cell 16
insert_at = 17  # after current cell 16
nb["cells"].insert(insert_at, md_compare_header)
nb["cells"].insert(insert_at + 1, mk_code(compare_load_code, output=compare_out))
nb["cells"].insert(insert_at + 2, md_compare_takeaway)

# --- 2) Update SPHERE placeholder in what is now cell 23 (was 20, shifted by +3) ---
# Find the cell whose source contains the SPHERE placeholder
sphere_idx = None
for i, c in enumerate(nb["cells"]):
    src = "".join(c["source"])
    if '"sysname": "agent"' in src and "aspects" in src and "..." in src:
        sphere_idx = i; break
assert sphere_idx is not None, "Could not locate SPHERE placeholder cell"

src = "".join(nb["cells"][sphere_idx]["source"])
old_block = '''  ```json
  {
    "sysname": "agent",
    "aspects": {
    ...
  ```'''
new_block = '''The full machine-readable JSON is committed under [`docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json`](../docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json). Inline summary:

  ```json
  {
    "sysname": "SAGE — Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement",
    "card_type": "intrinsic",
    "aspects": {
      "S": "Pedagogical tutor for AI-agent literacy; 18 skills under .claude/skills/; runtime is Streamlit + CLI on Anthropic SDK. In scope: question discipline, front-loading, answer-first on direct questions. Out of scope: tool-call correctness, factual accuracy of content.",
      "P": "Intrinsic: 3 persona-simulated learners (novice-curious, skeptical-engineer, fatigued-returner) + 7 hand-authored gold transcripts (4 positive, 3 negative). Extrinsic: 3 university students outside the team.",
      "H": {
        "metric_1": "Front-Loading Discipline (rule-based, evaluation/metrics/front_loading.py): per turn, ?-count <= 1 AND <= 4 sentences before first ?.",
        "metric_2": "Answer-First Adherence (LLM-as-judge, evaluation/metrics/answer_first.py): Haiku 4.5 classifier (yes_no | factual | open | not_a_question) then Sonnet 4.6 grader (answered_first | answered_and_followed_up | redirected_without_answer | non_answer)."
      },
      "E": {
        "final_agent_full_corpus":    { "front_loading": "23/53 (0.434)", "answer_first": "9/13 (0.692)" },
        "final_agent_simulated_only": { "front_loading": "9/19 (0.474)",  "answer_first": "7/10 (0.700)" },
        "4_16_agent_simulated_only":  { "front_loading": "9/19 (0.474)",  "answer_first": "8/8 (1.000)"  },
        "discriminative_validity":    "Authored positive (0.500) > negative (0.333) Front-Loading rate."
      },
      "R": [
        "Small corpus (19 simulated SAGE turns per agent version) makes per-persona rates fragile.",
        "Conversation-flow divergence: each agent version drives the persona LLM into different applicable-turn sets, confounding direct comparison on Metric 2.",
        "Single LLM grader (no human-judge alignment study yet)."
      ],
      "Empirical_strength": "medium"
    },
    "saved_artifacts": [
      "Experiment Results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json",
      "Experiment Results/agent-final-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json",
      "Experiment Results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json"
    ]
  }
  ```'''

assert old_block in src, "SPHERE placeholder block not found verbatim — aborting"
src = src.replace(old_block, new_block)
nb["cells"][sphere_idx]["source"] = src.splitlines(keepends=True)

# --- 3) Replace the trailing empty code cell with a Submission markdown cell ---
# Find the LAST cell — should be the empty code cell (originally cell 22)
last = nb["cells"][-1]
last_src = "".join(last["source"]).strip()
assert last_src == "" or last["cell_type"] == "code", "Trailing cell is not empty; refusing to touch it"

submission_md = '''# Submissions

The seven submission items from the assignment brief. Existing chat transcripts (Subjects 1–3 in the extrinsic section above) and intrinsic results in Step 3 are unchanged; this cell only adds the links and references the assignment asks for.

1. **Project specification** — `docs/architecture.md`, [`docs/technical-report.md`](../docs/technical-report.md). Agent contract: [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md).

2. **Prior iteration report (+ final-sprint addendum)** — [`docs/roadmap.md`](../docs/roadmap.md) for the prior plan; [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §3–§4](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md) for what *was* and *was not* accomplished after the 4-23 feedback.

3. **Completed final project evaluation (latest agent)** — this notebook's Step 3 cells above, plus the saved results files committed under [`Experiment Results/`](../Experiment%20Results/):
   - [`final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json`](../Experiment%20Results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json) — full run (7 authored + 3 simulated, 53 SAGE turns)
   - [`final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-summary.md`](../Experiment%20Results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-summary.md) — human-readable summary

4. **4-16 agent Experiment Results** — see "Final agent vs. 4-16 agent" section earlier in this notebook. Commit: [`7c5492d`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/commit/7c5492d7d71a7381b2ead3df453627963c296e7e) on branch `build/agent-2`. Saved results:
   - [`agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json`](../Experiment%20Results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json)
   - [`agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-summary.md`](../Experiment%20Results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-summary.md)
   - 4-16 system prompt fixture: [`evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt`](fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt)
   - 4-16 simulated transcripts: [`evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/`](fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/)

5. **3–4 page write-up (latest vs 4-16 comparison + future work + plan)** — [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](../docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md). §1–§3 cover per-metric, per-persona deltas with hypotheses for what changed and why; §4 covers ~1.5 pages of future work across three concrete proposals (mechanical-constraints block, expanded persona corpus, new routing metric) with design rationale and plans.

6. **GitHub repo** — <https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/> (final branch: [`AgentV2.1`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/tree/AgentV2.1)).

7. **3–4 page technical summary** — [`docs/technical-report.md`](../docs/technical-report.md).

---

**Reproducibility.** All numbers shown in this notebook are loaded from JSON committed under `Experiment Results/`, so the grader can read every table without re-running the harness. The full pipeline if a re-run is desired:

```bash
# Final agent — uses sage.prompts.SYSTEM_PROMPT
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --label final-agent

# 4-16 agent — same code, different system prompt
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all \\
  --sage-prompt evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt
mv evaluation/fixtures/simulated/*.json evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation \\
  --simulated-dir evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent \\
  --no-authored --label agent-4-16
```

53 unit tests under `evaluation/tests/` exercise parser, metrics, and judge cache without any API spend (`pytest evaluation/tests -v`).
'''

# Replace the trailing empty code cell with a markdown submission cell
nb["cells"][-1] = mk_md(submission_md)

json.dump(nb, open(nb_path, 'w'), indent=1)
print(f"V2 notebook updated. Total cells: {len(nb['cells'])}")
PY
