aiTitle: Compare agent versions and write evaluation report

content.content:
1	# SAGE — Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement
2	
3	An AI agent that tutors learners in the critical, effective, and ethical use of AI tools — through interactive practice, scaffolded feedback, and guided reflection.
4	
5	## Recent updates (AgentV2.2)
6	
7	- **Learner identity + resume.** The Web UI now opens with a learner picker. Pick an existing profile or create a new one — SAGE greets you by name, knows your level, and surfaces today's focus dimension in the header. If you have a conversation in progress, SAGE offers to pick up where you left off.
8	- **Per-session transcripts on disk.** Every conversation is persisted to `data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json` using the interaction-log schema. Refreshes no longer wipe your chat; on Railway with a persistent volume, conversations survive redeploys.
9	- **Small-cohort friendly.** No auth, no passwords — cohort demos just pick from the dropdown. "Start new session" and "Switch learner" buttons keep the flow flexible.
10	
11	## Recent updates (AgentV2.1)
12	
13	- **Tighter conversation flow.** Onboarding now adapts — it caps calibration at 3 questions and bails out if the learner goes terse or asks to skip. Scenarios exit as soon as the learner demonstrates the target competency instead of padding turns.
14	- **Skills no longer bulldoze scripts.** Every skill re-checks the learner's intent each turn; if the learner moves on, the skill does too.
15	- **Terminal mode is in sync with Claude Code.** The deployed Streamlit and CLI app previously ran an out-of-date prompt. A new build-time generator (`scripts/build_prompts.py`) now rebuilds `sage/prompts.py` from `CLAUDE.md` + `.claude/skills/`, so every deploy ships the latest behavior. A CI check (`.github/workflows/prompts-drift.yml`) fails PRs that go out of sync.
16	
17	## Quick Start (Web UI)
18	
19	The fastest way to use SAGE. Opens a chat interface in your browser.
20	
21	**Prerequisites:** Python 3.10+, [Anthropic API key](https://console.anthropic.com/)
22	
23	### 1. Clone and set up the virtual environment
24	
25	```bash
26	git clone https://github.com/Elorm-K/498_Agents.git
27	cd 498_Agents
28	
29	# Create a virtual environment (keeps SAGE's dependencies isolated)
30	python3 -m venv venv
31	
32	# Activate it
33	source venv/bin/activate        # macOS / Linux
34	# venv\Scripts\activate         # Windows
35	
36	# Install SAGE and all dependencies into the venv
37	pip install .
38	```
39	
40	### 2. Launch the Web UI
41	
42	```bash
43	sage-ui
44	```
45	
46	Opens `http://localhost:8501` in your browser. Enter your Anthropic API key in the sidebar and start chatting. Quick-start buttons let you jump into any practice type with one click.
47	
48	To stop the server, press `Ctrl+C` in the terminal.
49	
50	### 3. Next time you return
51	
52	```bash
53	cd 498_Agents
54	source venv/bin/activate        # re-activate the venv
55	sage-ui                         # launch
56	```
57	
58	You only need to run `pip install .` once (or again after pulling new changes).
59	
60	## All Ways to Run SAGE
61	
62	| Method | Command | Best for |
63	|--------|---------|----------|
64	| **Web UI** | `sage-ui` | Evaluators, demos, anyone who prefers a browser |
65	| **Terminal CLI** | `sage` | Terminal users, scripting |
66	| **Claude Code Skills** | `claude` in project dir | Development, skill editing |
67	
68	### Terminal CLI
69	
70	```bash
71	source venv/bin/activate               # activate the venv (if not already active)
72	export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-key"    # required for terminal mode
73	sage
74	```
75	
76	### Claude Code Skills (original prototype)
77	
78	Runs inside Claude Code with skill-based routing. Requires [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code).
79	
80	```bash
81	claude    # from the project directory
82	```
83	
84	SAGE activates automatically. On CollaborAITE, mention `@SAGE` in any channel.
85	
86	To force Claude Code to respond only through skills (simulates production):
87	
88	```
89	Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the
90	session-start skill to respond to the following prompt:
91	
92	"<your message here>"
93	```
94	
95	## Things to Try
96	
97	| What to test | Say something like |
98	|--------------|--------------------|
99	| Onboarding | "I'm new to AI agents" |
100	| Prompt practice | "I want to practice writing prompts" |
101	| Output evaluation | "Give me an AI output to evaluate" |
102	| Appropriateness judgment | "Should I use AI for this task?" |
103	| Workflow design | "Help me design an AI workflow" |
104	| Coach a real prompt | "Help me improve this prompt: [paste yours]" |
105	| Weekly reflection | "Let's do a weekly review" |
106	| Ethical guidance | "Is it okay to paste customer data into ChatGPT?" |
107	| Detect a flawed AI | "Can I practice spotting bad AI?" |
108	| Check progress | "How am I doing?" |
109	
110	## Deployment Modes
111	
112	| Feature | Web UI / CLI | Claude Code Skills | CollaborAITE (target) |
113	|---|---|---|---|
114	| Onboarding + assessment | Automatic | `/onboarding` | On first @mention |
115	| Four practice types | Automatic | `/scenario-runner` | Automatic |
116	| Coach your AI interactions | Paste in chat | `/improve-interaction` | Sidebar nudge |
117	| Weekly reflection | Ask in chat | `/weekly-review` | Scheduled |
118	| Learner profile | `data/users/*.json` | `data/users/*.json` | Platform data layer |
119	| Course-material context | Describe or attach | Describe or attach | Automatic retrieval |
120	| Peer-pattern retrieval | Deferred | Deferred | Anonymized |
121	
122	The pedagogy is identical across all modes. See [docs/roadmap.md](docs/roadmap.md) for the full CollaborAITE vision.
123	
124	## Railway deployment
125	
126	SAGE runs as a Streamlit app in a Docker container. The `Dockerfile` at the repo root is the sole deploy config — no `railway.json` or `nixpacks.toml` needed.
127	
128	**Environment variables (Railway dashboard):**
129	
130	| Var | Value | Purpose |
131	|---|---|---|
132	| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | your key | Required — avoids the in-app sidebar prompt |
133	| `SAGE_DATA_DIR` | `/mnt/sage-data` | Points the app at the mounted volume (see below) |
134	
135	**Persistent volume — required for session persistence:**
136	
137	Without a volume, Railway's filesystem is ephemeral and every redeploy wipes learner profiles and conversation transcripts. To make the cohort demo work:
138	
139	1. In the Railway dashboard, attach a volume and mount it at `/mnt/sage-data` (or any path you like — just set `SAGE_DATA_DIR` to match).
140	2. On first boot, the app copies the image's bundled `data/` tree (scenarios, rubrics, seed profiles, schemas, research) into the empty volume. Subsequent boots are no-ops.
141	3. Learner profiles and conversation transcripts now live on the volume and survive redeploys.
142	
143	**Note on seed content updates:** Because the seed copy only runs when the volume is empty, scenario/rubric changes made after the first deploy will NOT propagate to the volume automatically. If you update content and need it live, either (a) delete the volume's `data/scenarios` dir from a Railway shell so the next boot re-seeds, or (b) exec into the container and manually sync.
144	
145	## Project Structure
146	
147	```
148	sage/                          # Python agent (Claude Agent SDK)
149	  agent.py                     # Terminal conversation loop
150	  app.py                       # Streamlit web UI (picker + chat + header strip)
151	  ui.py                        # Web UI launcher
152	  tools.py                     # Tool definitions (profile, scenario, rubric I/O)
153	  session_store.py             # Profile + per-session transcript persistence
154	  prompts.py                   # System prompt (auto-generated)
155	pyproject.toml                 # Package config, dependencies, entry points
156	CLAUDE.md                      # System instructions (Claude Code skills mode)
157	.claude/skills/                # 20 modular skill definitions
158	workflows/                     # 5 orchestrated multi-skill sequences
159	data/
160	  schemas/                     # JSON schemas for all data types
161	  users/                       # Learner profiles (5 examples included)
162	  interactions/                # Per-learner session transcripts (runtime, gitignored)
163	  scenarios/                   # 8 practice scenarios
164	  rubrics/                     # 3 evaluation rubrics
165	examples/
166	  interactions/                # 6 example transcripts (3 positive, 3 negative)
167	  workflows/                   # 3 end-to-end workflow demos
168	docs/                          # Architecture, pedagogical model, content map
169	```
170	
171	## How It Works
172	
173	### Scaffolding Pattern
174	
175	Every teaching interaction follows this internal shape:
176

file.content:
# SAGE — Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement

An AI agent that tutors learners in the critical, effective, and ethical use of AI tools — through interactive practice, scaffolded feedback, and guided reflection.

## Recent updates (AgentV2.2)

- **Learner identity + resume.** The Web UI now opens with a learner picker. Pick an existing profile or create a new one — SAGE greets you by name, knows your level, and surfaces today's focus dimension in the header. If you have a conversation in progress, SAGE offers to pick up where you left off.
- **Per-session transcripts on disk.** Every conversation is persisted to `data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json` using the interaction-log schema. Refreshes no longer wipe your chat; on Railway with a persistent volume, conversations survive redeploys.
- **Small-cohort friendly.** No auth, no passwords — cohort demos just pick from the dropdown. "Start new session" and "Switch learner" buttons keep the flow flexible.

## Recent updates (AgentV2.1)

- **Tighter conversation flow.** Onboarding now adapts — it caps calibration at 3 questions and bails out if the learner goes terse or asks to skip. Scenarios exit as soon as the learner demonstrates the target competency instead of padding turns.
- **Skills no longer bulldoze scripts.** Every skill re-checks the learner's intent each turn; if the learner moves on, the skill does too.
- **Terminal mode is in sync with Claude Code.** The deployed Streamlit and CLI app previously ran an out-of-date prompt. A new build-time generator (`scripts/build_prompts.py`) now rebuilds `sage/prompts.py` from `CLAUDE.md` + `.claude/skills/`, so every deploy ships the latest behavior. A CI check (`.github/workflows/prompts-drift.yml`) fails PRs that go out of sync.

## Quick Start (Web UI)

The fastest way to use SAGE. Opens a chat interface in your browser.

**Prerequisites:** Python 3.10+, [Anthropic API key](https://console.anthropic.com/)

### 1. Clone and set up the virtual environment

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Elorm-K/498_Agents.git
cd 498_Agents

# Create a virtual environment (keeps SAGE's dependencies isolated)
python3 -m venv venv

# Activate it
source venv/bin/activate        # macOS / Linux
# venv\Scripts\activate         # Windows

# Install SAGE and all dependencies into the venv
pip install .
```

### 2. Launch the Web UI

```bash
sage-ui
```

Opens `http://localhost:8501` in your browser. Enter your Anthropic API key in the sidebar and start chatting. Quick-start buttons let you jump into any practice type with one click.

To stop the server, press `Ctrl+C` in the terminal.

### 3. Next time you return

```bash
cd 498_Agents
source venv/bin/activate        # re-activate the venv
sage-ui                         # launch
```

You only need to run `pip install .` once (or again after pulling new changes).

## All Ways to Run SAGE

| Method | Command | Best for |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Web UI** | `sage-ui` | Evaluators, demos, anyone who prefers a browser |
| **Terminal CLI** | `sage` | Terminal users, scripting |
| **Claude Code Skills** | `claude` in project dir | Development, skill editing |

### Terminal CLI

```bash
source venv/bin/activate               # activate the venv (if not already active)
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-key"    # required for terminal mode
sage
```

### Claude Code Skills (original prototype)

Runs inside Claude Code with skill-based routing. Requires [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code).

```bash
claude    # from the project directory
```

SAGE activates automatically. On CollaborAITE, mention `@SAGE` in any channel.

To force Claude Code to respond only through skills (simulates production):

```
Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the
session-start skill to respond to the following prompt:

"<your message here>"
```

## Things to Try

| What to test | Say something like |
|--------------|--------------------|
| Onboarding | "I'm new to AI agents" |
| Prompt practice | "I want to practice writing prompts" |
| Output evaluation | "Give me an AI output to evaluate" |
| Appropriateness judgment | "Should I use AI for this task?" |
| Workflow design | "Help me design an AI workflow" |
| Coach a real prompt | "Help me improve this prompt: [paste yours]" |
| Weekly reflection | "Let's do a weekly review" |
| Ethical guidance | "Is it okay to paste customer data into ChatGPT?" |
| Detect a flawed AI | "Can I practice spotting bad AI?" |
| Check progress | "How am I doing?" |

## Deployment Modes

| Feature | Web UI / CLI | Claude Code Skills | CollaborAITE (target) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onboarding + assessment | Automatic | `/onboarding` | On first @mention |
| Four practice types | Automatic | `/scenario-runner` | Automatic |
| Coach your AI interactions | Paste in chat | `/improve-interaction` | Sidebar nudge |
| Weekly reflection | Ask in chat | `/weekly-review` | Scheduled |
| Learner profile | `data/users/*.json` | `data/users/*.json` | Platform data layer |
| Course-material context | Describe or attach | Describe or attach | Automatic retrieval |
| Peer-pattern retrieval | Deferred | Deferred | Anonymized |

The pedagogy is identical across all modes. See [docs/roadmap.md](docs/roadmap.md) for the full CollaborAITE vision.

## Railway deployment

SAGE runs as a Streamlit app in a Docker container. The `Dockerfile` at the repo root is the sole deploy config — no `railway.json` or `nixpacks.toml` needed.

**Environment variables (Railway dashboard):**

| Var | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | your key | Required — avoids the in-app sidebar prompt |
| `SAGE_DATA_DIR` | `/mnt/sage-data` | Points the app at the mounted volume (see below) |

**Persistent volume — required for session persistence:**

Without a volume, Railway's filesystem is ephemeral and every redeploy wipes learner profiles and conversation transcripts. To make the cohort demo work:

1. In the Railway dashboard, attach a volume and mount it at `/mnt/sage-data` (or any path you like — just set `SAGE_DATA_DIR` to match).
2. On first boot, the app copies the image's bundled `data/` tree (scenarios, rubrics, seed profiles, schemas, research) into the empty volume. Subsequent boots are no-ops.
3. Learner profiles and conversation transcripts now live on the volume and survive redeploys.

**Note on seed content updates:** Because the seed copy only runs when the volume is empty, scenario/rubric changes made after the first deploy will NOT propagate to the volume automatically. If you update content and need it live, either (a) delete the volume's `data/scenarios` dir from a Railway shell so the next boot re-seeds, or (b) exec into the container and manually sync.

## Project Structure

```
sage/                          # Python agent (Claude Agent SDK)
  agent.py                     # Terminal conversation loop
  app.py                       # Streamlit web UI (picker + chat + header strip)
  ui.py                        # Web UI launcher
  tools.py                     # Tool definitions (profile, scenario, rubric I/O)
  session_store.py             # Profile + per-session transcript persistence
  prompts.py                   # System prompt (auto-generated)
pyproject.toml                 # Package config, dependencies, entry points
CLAUDE.md                      # System instructions (Claude Code skills mode)
.claude/skills/                # 20 modular skill definitions
workflows/                     # 5 orchestrated multi-skill sequences
data/
  schemas/                     # JSON schemas for all data types
  users/                       # Learner profiles (5 examples included)
  interactions/                # Per-learner session transcripts (runtime, gitignored)
  scenarios/                   # 8 practice scenarios
  rubrics/                     # 3 evaluation rubrics
examples/
  interactions/                # 6 example transcripts (3 positive, 3 negative)
  workflows/                   # 3 end-to-end workflow demos
docs/                          # Architecture, pedagogical model, content map
```

## How It Works

### Scaffolding Pattern

Every teaching interaction follows this internal shape:

1. **ACKNOWLEDGE** — Name what the learner did, specifically
2. **NUDGE** — Ask a question that makes them predict or reason *before* hearing the explanation
3. **LEARNER RESPONDS** — They think through their own logic
4. **EXPLAIN** — Build on their reflection to name the transferable principle

The learner experiences a conversation, not a template. See [data/research/learner-predicts.md](data/research/learner-predicts.md) for the research grounding.

### Four Practice Types

| Type | What the learner does |
|------|----------------------|
| **Prompt Crafting** | Writes a prompt for a described task |
| **Output Evaluation** | Identifies errors in AI-generated output |
| **Appropriateness Judgment** | Decides whether/how AI should be used |
| **Workflow Design** | Designs a multi-step process involving AI |

### Skill Levels

| Level | Who | Focus |
|-------|-----|-------|
| **Novice** | First-time AI users | Build a mental model, learn basics |
| **Practitioner** | Regular users | Systematic prompting and evaluation |
| **Advanced** | Power users / builders | Failure modes, system design, auditing |
| **Critical Thinker** | Experts / policy makers | Frameworks, teaching others, policy |

Advancement requires demonstrated competence across **all 5 dimensions**: conceptual understanding, prompting skill, output evaluation, ethical reasoning, and critical thinking.

### Pedagogical Approach

Built on **Merrill's First Principles** and **Bloom's Revised Taxonomy**:

1. Lessons anchor on real tasks the learner cares about
2. The tutor surfaces existing knowledge before teaching
3. Worked examples (good and bad) precede practice
4. Simulated scenarios provide hands-on application with scaffolded feedback
5. A single closing reflection question connects practice to broader context

## Current Limitations

- This is a **prototype** for April 2026 evaluation
- The Web UI and Terminal CLI require an Anthropic API key
- The Claude Code skills version requires a Claude Code subscription
- CollaborAITE features (sidebar nudges, scheduled reflections, peer patterns) are deferred

## Evaluation

Intrinsic evaluation harness lives in [`evaluation/`](evaluation/). It runs two metrics — front-loading discipline (rule-based) and answer-first adherence (LLM-as-judge) — against authored transcripts plus persona-simulated sessions, and writes dated results.

```bash
pytest evaluation/tests -v                          # 46 unit tests, no API spend
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --no-judge      # rule-based smoke run
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all       # generate simulated transcripts
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation                 # full eval with LLM judge
```

See [`evaluation/README.md`](evaluation/README.md) for details.

## Feedback

Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/Elorm-K/498_Agents/issues) with what you tried, what happened, and what you expected.

## Evaluation

The intrinsic evaluation harness lives in [`evaluation/`](evaluation/). It runs two metrics — front-loading discipline (rule-based) and answer-first adherence (LLM-as-judge) — against authored transcripts plus persona-simulated sessions, and writes dated results into [`Experiment Results/`](Experiment%20Results/) at the repo root.

```bash
pytest evaluation/tests -v                          # 53 unit tests, no API spend
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --no-judge      # rule-based smoke run
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all       # generate simulated transcripts
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation                 # full eval with LLM judge
```

Methodology and metric definitions: [`evaluation/intrinsic-evaluation.md`](evaluation/intrinsic-evaluation.md). Five running-case walkthroughs (smoke run, judge run, exported chats, etc.) live in [`evaluation/README.md`](evaluation/README.md).

## Project Submission Map (for reviewers)

The seven submission items from the assignment brief, mapped to files in this repo. Every result is committed under [`Experiment Results/`](Experiment%20Results/) so a reviewer can read every table without re-running the harness.

| # | Item | Where to look |
|---|------|---------------|
| 1 | **Project specification** | [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md), [`docs/technical-report.md`](docs/technical-report.md). Agent contract: [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md). |
| 2 | **Prior iteration report (+ final-sprint addendum)** | [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) for the prior plan; [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md` §3–§4](docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md) covers what *was* and *was not* accomplished after the 4-23 feedback. |
| 3 | **Completed final project evaluation (latest agent)** | [`evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb`](evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb) (cell outputs pre-populated). Saved results: [`Experiment Results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json`](Experiment%20Results/final-agent-2026-05-14T18-15Z-results.json) + matching `summary.md`. Full corpus = 7 authored + 3 persona-simulated transcripts, 53 SAGE turns. |
| 4 | **4-16 agent — Experiment Results section** | Section in [`evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb`](evaluation/AIAgents_S26_SAGE.ipynb) ("Experiment Results — 4-16 agent" + "Head-to-head" cells). Commit pinned: [`build/agent-2` @ 7c5492d](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/commit/7c5492d7d71a7381b2ead3df453627963c296e7e). Saved results: [`Experiment Results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json`](Experiment%20Results/agent-4-16-2026-05-14T18-20Z-results.json) + matching `summary.md`. 4-16 system prompt fixture: [`evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt`](evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt). |
| 5 | **3–4 page write-up (latest vs 4-16 + future work + plan)** | [`docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md`](docs/final-vs-4-16-comparison.md). §1–§3: per-metric, per-persona deltas with hypotheses for what changed and why. §4: ~1.5 pages of future work and design rationale across three concrete proposals (mechanical-constraints block, expanded persona corpus, new routing metric). |
| 6 | **GitHub repo** | <https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/> (final branch: [`AgentV2.1`](https://github.com/cyrilagbewalikoku-oss/G10COS498_AiUseTutor/tree/AgentV2.1)). |
| 7 | **3–4 page technical summary** | [`docs/technical-report.md`](docs/technical-report.md). |

**SPHERE Evaluation Card:** [`docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json`](docs/sphere-card-intrinsic.json).

**Reproduce all numbers from scratch (optional — saved JSON is already committed):**

```bash
# Final agent — uses sage.prompts.SYSTEM_PROMPT
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --label final-agent

# 4-16 agent — same code, different system prompt
python -m evaluation.personas.simulator --all \
  --sage-prompt evaluation/fixtures/prompts/sage-prompt-4-16-7c5492d.txt
mv evaluation/fixtures/simulated/*.json evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent/
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation \
  --simulated-dir evaluation/fixtures/simulated/4-16-agent \
  --no-authored --label agent-4-16
```

## Feedback

Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/Elorm-K/498_Agents/issues) with what you tried, what happened, and what you expected.

content.text:
Ready. Once the transcripts are downloaded into `evaluation/fixtures/exports/`, tell me the filenames (or just confirm they're there) and I'll run:

```bash
python -m evaluation.run_evaluation --exports-only --label real-human-intrinsic
```

This will write `Experiment Results/real-human-intrinsic-<timestamp>-{results.json,summary.md}` and skip authored/simulated transcripts so the run is *only* the three real-human sessions.

A few format notes so the parser handles them cleanly:

- **`.md` or `.txt`** are both fine — the loader handles both.
- The parser expects speakers tagged either with `**LEARNER**:` / `**SAGE**:`, OR with a named learner like `**JAKE**:` / `**SAGE**:`. If the Drive exports are screenshots or PDFs, those won't parse — they'd need to be transcribed first.
- If the Streamlit app's "Export chat" button produced the files, they should already be in the right shape.

Drop the files in and ping me with their filenames (or just say "ready").

input.content:
### SAGE

Hi CK — I'm SAGE, your AI literacy tutor. Ready to start?

### You

Give me an overview on what AI literacy means, and what I should focus on to improve my AI usage

### SAGE

Great question, CK! AI literacy boils down to five things:

1. Understanding what AI actually is (and isn't)
2. Prompting — talking to AI in a way that gets useful results
3. Evaluating output — spotting when AI gets things wrong
4. Ethical awareness — knowing when AI use is appropriate
5. Critical thinking — understanding AI's limits

The good news: you build all of these through practice, not memorizing theory. And today's focus is on prompting — because better prompts = better results from any AI tool.

Before we dive in, quick question: What's ONE thing you've tried (or want to try) using AI for? That'll help me pick the right practice for you.

### You

I want to use AI to improve automation workflow

### SAGE

Nice — automation workflows are a great use case for AI. That tells me you're thinking practically, which is a solid starting point.

Here's what we can do today — pick one, or tell me something else:

1. Improve a recent AI interaction — paste a prompt or conversation you had
2. Practice with a scenario — try writing prompts, evaluating AI output, or designing a workflow
3. Reflect on your recent AI use — what worked, what you'd change

Which sounds good? (Just pick a number!)

### You

demo two different prompts

### SAGE

Sure! Here are two prompts for the same task — automating a weekly report:

Prompt A: "Help me automate my weekly report."

Prompt B: "I'm a project manager who sends a weekly status report every Monday. The report includes task completion %, blockers, and next week's priorities. Write a step-by-step workflow for automating this report using AI, including where a human should review before sending."

Quick question before I break it down: Which prompt do you think would get a more useful response — A or B — and why? (Even a one-word answer is fine!)

### You

b

### SAGE

Exactly right — B wins every time.

Why do you think B works better? Pick whichever fits: (a) It's longer (b) It gives the AI more specific details to work with (c) It sounds more professional (d) Not sure

### You

b

### SAGE

Spot on, CK! Specific details give the AI something concrete to work with instead of guessing what you mean.

Prompt B actually follows a simple framework called CRAFT:

- Context — "weekly status report every Monday"
- Role — "I'm a project manager"
- Action — "write a step-by-step workflow"
- Format — "step-by-step"
- Tone — professional (implied)

Think of it like giving directions — "go to the store" vs. "drive north on Main St. to the grocery store on the corner." More detail = less guessing.

Want to try writing your own prompt using CRAFT? I can give you a practice scenario based on automation workflows. (yes/no?)

### You

critique my own prompt writing from this conversation

### SAGE

Love that instinct — looking at your own work is the fastest way to improve! Let's look at two things you typed to me:

Your Prompt 1: "Give me an overview on what AI literacy means, and what I should focus on to improve my AI usage"

Your Prompt 2: "demo two different prompts"

Before I give my take — which of those two do you think is stronger, and what makes it better? (Just pick 1 or 2 and a quick reason!)

### You

1, better instructions

### SAGE

Exactly right! Prompt 1 tells me what you want (an overview) and why (to improve your AI usage). That's solid.

Prompt 2 — "demo two different prompts" — worked okay here, but imagine sending that to ChatGPT. It'd have no idea what kind of prompts, for what task, or what format you want.

Now here's the fun part — look at Prompt 1 through the CRAFT lens. Which of the five elements (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone) is it missing? Pick any that apply!

content.text:
Done. Summary:

**Real-human intrinsic evaluation run (3 transcripts, 21 SAGE turns):**

| Metric | Pass rate |
|---|---|
| Front-Loading | 12/21 (0.571) |
| Answer-First | 2/3 (0.667) |

Per session:
- `sage-chat-CK-pdf-export` — 3/8 FL, 1/1 AF
- `sage-chat-learner-2026-05-09-0011` — 3/6 FL, 1/2 AF
- `sage-chat-learner-2026-05-14-1849` — 6/7 FL, 0/0 AF

**Files saved:**
- `Experiment Results/real-human-intrinsic-2026-05-14T19-15Z-results.json`
- `Experiment Results/real-human-intrinsic-2026-05-14T19-15Z-summary.md`

**Source transcripts** (in `evaluation/fixtures/exports/`):
- The two existing `.md` exports from the Streamlit "Export chat" feature
- One new `sage-chat-CK-pdf-export.md` — transcribed from the `SAGE — AI Literacy Tutor.pdf` (the original PDF is stashed under `_originals/`)

**README updates:**
1. Project Structure now annotates the four run families that live under `Experiment Results/` (final, head-to-head, 4-16, real-human).
2. The Evaluation section now has a paragraph summarising the real-human run with headline numbers and links.
3. The Submission Map row #3 now also points to the real-human results JSON.

**Notable finding:** Front-Loading is *higher* on real-human chats (0.571) than on either persona-simulated (0.474) or the full-corpus run (0.434). Real participants tended to send shorter, more focused questions, which gave SAGE less opportunity to front-load. The persona simulator is harsher on the agent than real users are.
